title: AcyclicUnderIntervention
lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_3 part A: if `G` is acyclic then so is
`G.hardInterventionOn W`, for *any* `W : Set α`. Mirrors the first
half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_10 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 311).

We split the LN's single `\Rem` into two theorems because the
acyclicity and topological-order halves carry different
hypotheses, and different downstream consumers pattern-match on
different halves. `claim_3_4` ("hard interventions commute") and
the iSCM uniqueness theory of chapters 8 -- 10 need acyclicity of
the intervened graph *alone* -- they have no named topological
order on hand, so a bundled conjunction would force them either
to project, or to drag in the `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition that
part B (`isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn`) requires but
part A does not. The downstream consumers of part B in
`do_calculus.tex` / `id_algorithm.tex` carry a named `<` through
long proofs and likewise prefer the relation-level form on its
own, without the acyclicity conjunct.

No `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition appears here because every
directed edge of `G.hardInterventionOn W` is a directed edge of
`G` (by `mem_hardInterventionOn_E` in `HardInterventionOn.lean`),
so directed walks in the intervened graph lift to walks of the
same length in `G` and any cycle would lift to a cycle in `G`.
Spurious vertices in `W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` participate in no edges
at all (by `G.E_subset` on the source / `G.L_subset` on both
endpoints), so they cannot host a cycle either. For the same
reason no `[Finite α]` instance is required -- the walk-lifting
argument is purely structural (edges shrink under intervention),
and adding finiteness would block downstream use in iSCM
chapters working over not-yet-finitised vertex types. `W` is an
*explicit* binder because consumers (claim_3_4, claim_3_5,
iSCMs) typically have a specific `W` in mind that is not
unifiable from elsewhere, while `G` is implicit because it is
recovered from `(G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic`. The name
follows Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention --
the `IsAcyclic` predicate first, then the `hardInterventionOn`
construction we are showing preserves it. -/
theorem isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} (W : Set α) (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_3_proof_AcyclicUnderIntervention.tex` Part A.
  -- A non-trivial directed walk `v → ⋯ → v` in `G.hardInterventionOn W`
  -- lifts step-by-step (via `walkLiftHardInterventionOn`) to a non-trivial
  -- directed walk in `G`. Its first step witnesses `v ∈ G` via `G.E_subset`
  -- (def_3_1), contradicting `G.IsAcyclic`.
  rintro v _hv ⟨π, h_dir, h_pos⟩
  cases π with
  | nil _ => simp at h_pos
  | cons s p =>
    cases s with
    | forward h_e =>
      have h_e_G : (v, _) ∈ G.E := h_e.1
      have hv_G : v ∈ G :=
        CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_e_G)).1
      refine h v hv_G
        ⟨walkLiftHardInterventionOn (Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p), ?_, ?_⟩
      · exact walkLiftHardInterventionOn_isDirected _ h_dir
      · rw [walkLiftHardInterventionOn_length]; exact h_pos
    | backward _ => simp at h_dir
    | bidir _ => simp at h_dir

/-- claim_3_3 part B: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G`, then `r` is also a topological order of
`G.hardInterventionOn W`. Mirrors the second half of the `\Rem`
immediately after def_3_10 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 311); see
`isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` above for the rationale behind
splitting that `\Rem` into two theorems and for the downstream
consumers of the acyclicity half.

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition that was *absent* in part A is
*essential* here: `IsTopologicalOrder G' r` quantifies its four
fields (`irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` / `parent_lt`) over
the nodes of `G'`, and `(G.hardInterventionOn W).J ∪
(G.hardInterventionOn W).V = (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)` collapses to
`G.J ∪ G.V` only when `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`. Without the precondition
a vertex `v ∈ W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` would be a node of the intervened
graph that `hr.irrefl` / `hr.trichotomous` say nothing about,
breaking those fields in general. Part A escaped this issue
because its walk-lifting argument only touches
`(G.hardInterventionOn W).E ⊆ G.E`, never the node set of the
intervened graph.

`W` and `r` are both implicit because they are unifiable from the
hypotheses (`hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and
`hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r`) and from the conclusion
`.IsTopologicalOrder r`; downstream callers in
`do_calculus.tex` / `id_algorithm.tex` carry a fixed named `<`
through long proofs and pass it through `hr`, so they never need
to spell `r` (or `W`) at the call site -- they simply write
`isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn hW hr`. `G` is implicit
for the same reason it is in part A. Naming follows Mathlib's
`<conclusion>_<construction>` convention, consistent with part A
and with the `IsTopologicalOrder` predicate's own naming in
`Section3_1/TopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} {r : α → α → Prop}
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsTopologicalOrder r := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_3_proof_AcyclicUnderIntervention.tex` Part B.
  -- (Step 1) Under `hW`, the node sets of `G` and `G.hardInterventionOn W`
  -- agree: `(G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W) = G.J ∪ G.V`. (Steps 2 & 3) The four
  -- `IsTopologicalOrder` fields then transport directly from `hr`, with
  -- `parent_lt` additionally using the edge inclusion
  -- `(G.hardInterventionOn W).E ⊆ G.E` (`mem_hardInterventionOn_E`).
  have hnodes : ∀ v, v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W ↔ v ∈ G := by
    intro v
    simp only [CDMG.mem_iff, hardInterventionOn_J, hardInterventionOn_V,
      Set.mem_union, Set.mem_diff]
    refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
    · rintro ((hJ | hW') | ⟨hV, _⟩)
      · exact Or.inl hJ
      · exact hW hW'
      · exact Or.inr hV
    · rintro (hJ | hV)
      · exact Or.inl (Or.inl hJ)
      · by_cases hW' : v ∈ W
        · exact Or.inl (Or.inr hW')
        · exact Or.inr ⟨hV, hW'⟩
  refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- irrefl: node set agrees, transport from `hr.irrefl`.
    intro v hv
    exact hr.irrefl v ((hnodes v).mp hv)
  · -- trans: transport via `hr.trans`.
    intro v hv w hw x hx
    exact hr.trans v ((hnodes v).mp hv) w ((hnodes w).mp hw) x ((hnodes x).mp hx)
  · -- trichotomous: transport via `hr.trichotomous`.
    intro v hv w hw
    exact hr.trichotomous v ((hnodes v).mp hv) w ((hnodes w).mp hw)
  · -- parent_lt: edge inclusion + `G.E_subset` reduce to `hr.parent_lt`.
    intro v w hvw
    obtain ⟨_, h_edge⟩ := hvw
    have h_edge_G : (v, w) ∈ G.E := h_edge.1
    have hv_G : v ∈ G :=
      CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_edge_G)).1
    exact hr.parent_lt ⟨hv_G, h_edge_G⟩
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} (W : Set α) (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic := by

  rintro v _hv ⟨π, h_dir, h_pos⟩
  cases π with
  | nil _ => simp at h_pos
  | cons s p =>
    cases s with
    | forward h_e =>
      have h_e_G : (v, _) ∈ G.E := h_e.1
      have hv_G : v ∈ G :=
        CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_e_G)).1
      refine h v hv_G
        ⟨walkLiftHardInterventionOn (Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p), ?_, ?_⟩
      · exact walkLiftHardInterventionOn_isDirected _ h_dir
      · rw [walkLiftHardInterventionOn_length]; exact h_pos
    | backward _ => simp at h_dir
    | bidir _ => simp at h_dir

theorem isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} {r : α → α → Prop}
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsTopologicalOrder r := by

  have hnodes : ∀ v, v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W ↔ v ∈ G := by
    intro v
    simp only [CDMG.mem_iff, hardInterventionOn_J, hardInterventionOn_V,
      Set.mem_union, Set.mem_diff]
    refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
    · rintro ((hJ | hW') | ⟨hV, _⟩)
      · exact Or.inl hJ
      · exact hW hW'
      · exact Or.inr hV
    · rintro (hJ | hV)
      · exact Or.inl (Or.inl hJ)
      · by_cases hW' : v ∈ W
        · exact Or.inl (Or.inr hW')
        · exact Or.inr ⟨hV, hW'⟩
  refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  ·
    intro v hv
    exact hr.irrefl v ((hnodes v).mp hv)
  ·
    intro v hv w hw x hx
    exact hr.trans v ((hnodes v).mp hv) w ((hnodes w).mp hw) x ((hnodes x).mp hx)
  ·
    intro v hv w hw
    exact hr.trichotomous v ((hnodes v).mp hv) w ((hnodes w).mp hw)
  ·
    intro v w hvw
    obtain ⟨_, h_edge⟩ := hvw
    have h_edge_G : (v, w) ∈ G.E := h_edge.1
    have hv_G : v ∈ G :=
      CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_edge_G)).1
    exact hr.parent_lt ⟨hv_G, h_edge_G⟩
lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding of this remark is split into two theorems, both living in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace and quantifying over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*` — the LN's silent "let $G$ be a CDMG over some carrier". The intervention set is `W : Set α`, matching the LN's $W$ in $G_{\doit(W)}$; in Lean the intervened graph is written `G.hardInterventionOn W`.

Part A, `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn`, takes a CDMG `G : CDMG α` (implicit, recovered from the conclusion's `(G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic`), the set `W : Set α` (explicit), and a hypothesis `h : G.IsAcyclic`. It concludes `(G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic`. There is *no* `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition — the design notes below explain why.

Part B, `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn`, takes `G`, `W`, and a binary relation `r : α → α → Prop` playing the role of the LN's $<$, all implicit. The two hypotheses are `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` (the precondition that part A escapes) and `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r`. The conclusion is `(G.hardInterventionOn W).IsTopologicalOrder r` — that the *same* relation `r` is again a topological order, now on the intervened graph.

Two Mathlib idioms are worth flagging. First, `Set.Subset` (`W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`) together with the set-builder operations `G.J ∪ G.V` and `G.V \ W` are pure Mathlib set theory; the LN-level membership $v \in G_{\doit(W)}$ unfolds via def 3.10 to the union $(J \cup W) \cup (V \setminus W)$. Second, `IsTopologicalOrder` (def 3.8) is a *relation-level* predicate — a property of a specific `r`, not the existential "there exists some topological order" — so the LN phrase "a topological order for $G$ is also one for $G_{\doit(W)}$" most naturally reads as the transport asserted here: the same `r` works for both graphs. `IsAcyclic` (def 3.6) is the standard "no node admits a non-trivial directed walk to itself".

In shape: part A takes one hypothesis (acyclicity) and concludes one predicate. Part B takes two hypotheses and transports a four-field structure (`irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` / `parent_lt`) from `G` to `G.hardInterventionOn W` field by field, with the first three falling out of a node-set agreement step and `parent_lt` additionally using the directed-edge inclusion under intervention.
design_choices:
**The LN's single `\Rem` is split into two theorems with different hypotheses.** Forré–Mooij bundle the two conclusions — "$G_{\doit(W)}$ is acyclic" and "a topological order for $G$ is also one for $G_{\doit(W)}$" — under one paragraph. The Lean encoding splits them because they carry different preconditions and because their downstream consumers want different halves. The next claim of the chapter ("hard interventions commute") and the unique-solution theory of intervened structural causal models in later chapters need acyclicity of the intervened graph *without* any named topological order on hand; the do-calculus and identification-algorithm soundness proofs carry a fixed named $<$ through long arguments and want the relation-level form *without* an acyclicity conjunct. A single bundled conjunction would force every caller either to project, or to drag in a precondition the other half does not need.

**Part A takes no `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition; part B does.** The asymmetry is structural rather than cosmetic. The acyclicity argument only ever uses the edge inclusion $E_{\doit(W)} \subseteq E$: any directed walk in the intervened graph lifts step-by-step to a directed walk of the same length in $G$, so a hypothetical cycle in $G_{\doit(W)}$ would be a cycle in $G$. Spurious vertices in $W \setminus (J \cup V)$ get promoted to inputs but participate in no edges at all (by the CDMG invariants that say every edge has its endpoints inside $J \cup V$), so they cannot host a cycle either. The topological-order argument, by contrast, transports the four `IsTopologicalOrder` fields by instantiating their quantifiers on the *node set* of the intervened graph, namely $(J \cup W) \cup (V \setminus W)$, which only collapses to $J \cup V$ under $W \subseteq J \cup V$. Without that precondition a vertex in $W \setminus (J \cup V)$ would sit in the intervened graph's node set but be unconstrained by `hr.irrefl` / `hr.trichotomous`, breaking those fields in general.

**No finiteness assumption on `α`.** Acyclicity-preservation in part A is a structural walk-lifting argument that never enumerates vertices; the topological-order transport in part B is a quantifier-level manipulation of the four fields of `IsTopologicalOrder`. Neither needs a `[Finite α]` instance, and adding one would block downstream use in later chapters where vertex types are real-valued and not yet known to be finite at the point of application.

**`W` is explicit in part A, implicit in part B.** Part A's typical callers hold a specific intervention set in mind that is not unifiable from anywhere else in the call, so making `W` explicit lets them write `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn W h` directly. In part B, `W` is recoverable by unification from the precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, so it is left implicit; downstream callers carrying a named $<$ through long proofs simply write `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn hW hr` without ever spelling `W` or `r`. `G` is implicit in both halves, recovered from the conclusion.

**Names follow Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention.** `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` reads "the `IsAcyclic` predicate, applied to the `hardInterventionOn` construction"; `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn` does the same for `IsTopologicalOrder`. The convention scales to subsequent rows on this construction, signalling at a glance "a fact about `hardInterventionOn`".
